intro: The clearest difference between Backtrace and Crashpad is pricing — Backtrace is freemium (has a free tier), while Crashpad is free. Beyond that, lock-in — Backtrace has medium lock-in (Crash reports exportable), whereas Crashpad has low lock-in (Open-source Chromium crash handler). Backtrace fits mobile; Crashpad fits native apps needing proven minidump capture across OSes. On the strengths side, Backtrace is cited for mobile and web apps in production, while Crashpad is cited for battle-tested in Chrome across macOS, Windows, Linux. The honest trade-off: Backtrace is the wrong call for pre-launch, Simple projects; Crashpad is the wrong call for teams wanting a turnkey crash reporting product; Backtrace users flag Pre-launch/beta apps, and Crashpad users flag library, not a backend or UI. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Backtrace at 4/5 and Crashpad at 3/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 5/5 respectively.
quick_take: Backtrace is for mobile; Crashpad is for native apps needing proven minidump capture; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Backtrace if your use case is mobile, and a freemium (has a free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than free.
verdict_b: Choose Crashpad if your use case is native apps needing proven minidump capture across OSes, and a free pricing shape fits your budget better than freemium (has a free tier).

faq.a: On listed pricing Crashpad is free, whereas Backtrace follows a freemium model, so Crashpad is cheaper on the sticker. Factor in self-hosting and maintenance time before concluding which is actually cheaper for your team. Check both pricing pages for current tiers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Backtrace to Crashpad?
faq.a: Migrating from Backtrace to Crashpad is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Moving off Backtrace is harder because of medium lock-in (Crash reports exportable). Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Backtrace is rated 4/5 and Crashpad 3/5, so Backtrace edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Crashpad a good alternative to Backtrace?
faq.a: Crashpad is a reasonable alternative to Backtrace when Crashpad fits native apps needing proven minidump capture across OSes. If your use case matches Backtrace's sweet spot — mobile — staying with Backtrace is probably safer. Backtrace itself is not meant for pre-launch, Simple projects, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
